title: CC Exercises — CCV2 & Cloud Deployment
description: Hands-on exercises covering the manifest.json, build/deploy pipeline, Cloud Portal operations, environment-specific properties, and release management.

sections.title: Exercise 1 — Write & Validate a manifest.json
sections.content:
Objective

Create a production-ready manifest.json from scratch, covering all required sections, and validate it against CCV2 requirements.

Steps

  1. Create a new manifest.json in your project's root directory.
  2. Define the commerceSuiteVersion — look up the latest supported version from SAP's documentation.
  3. Define the extensions array. Include:
    • All required platform extensions for your storefront type (e.g. commercewebservices, cmsocc, ycommercewebservices, acceleratorservices, etc.).
    • Your custom extensions (the ones from the Tier 1-5 exercises).
    • Spartacus-related extensions if applicable (smarteditwebservices, cmsoccaddon).
  4. Define aspects:
    • api (or accstorefront) — for web traffic.
    • backgroundProcessing — for CronJobs and batch imports.
    • admin — for Backoffice and HAC.
  5. Define properties section with:
    • A global property: log4j2.logger.com.myextension.level = INFO
    • A dev-only property: same logger set to DEBUG, with persona = development.
    • A production-only property: cronjob.timertask.loadonstartup = true with persona = production, for backgroundProcessing aspect only.
    • A storefront-only property: cronjob.timertask.loadonstartup = false with persona matching the api/storefront aspect.
  6. Optionally, add a useConfig section referencing an external properties file: config/custom.properties.

Validation

Review your manifest against the SAP documentation's manifest schema. Verify: every extension you depend on is listed (unlisted = excluded from the build), aspects are correctly named, properties don't conflict (check for the same key with overlapping personas), and the commerceSuiteVersion matches your code compatibility. If you have CCV2 access, trigger a build and verify it succeeds.

sections.title: Exercise 2 — Cloud Portal Build & Deploy Simulation
sections.content:
Objective

Walk through the complete build-deploy lifecycle using the Cloud Portal (or simulate it on paper if you don't have CCV2 access).

Steps (with CCV2 access)

  1. Push your code (with manifest.json) to the connected Git repository.
  2. In the Cloud Portal → Builds, trigger a new build from your branch. Watch the build log stream.
  3. If the build fails: read the log, identify the error (missing extension dependency, compilation error, manifest issue), fix it, and rebuild.
  4. If the build succeeds: note the build code. Navigate to the dev environment.
  5. Trigger a deployment to the dev environment using Update mode (not Initialize — preserve data).
  6. Monitor the deployment status. Once complete, verify the application is running by accessing the storefront or HAC endpoint.
  7. Verify your custom extensions are loaded: in HAC → Platform → Extensions, search for your extension name.

Steps (paper exercise — no CCV2 access)

  1. Document the build-deploy pipeline as a flowchart: Git push → Build trigger → Build engine (clones repo, resolves platform version, compiles, packages Docker image) → Build artifact registered → Deploy to environment (select build + mode) → Database migration → Application start → Health check.
  2. For each step, list what can go wrong and how you would diagnose it (build log, deployment log, application log, HAC health check).
  3. Design a CI/CD pipeline using the Cloud Portal API: write pseudocode for a pipeline that triggers a build, polls status, deploys to dev, runs smoke tests, and on success deploys to staging with a manual approval gate before production.

Validation

Either a working deployment in CCV2 or a comprehensive paper design that covers the full lifecycle, error scenarios, and CI/CD automation.

sections.title: Exercise 3 — Environment-Specific Configuration Across Tenants
sections.content:
Objective

Design and document a complete property configuration strategy for a three-environment deployment (dev/staging/production), ensuring correct values in each environment.

Steps

  1. Create a table with the following columns: Property Key, Dev Value, Staging Value, Production Value, Aspect (all/api/backgroundProcessing/admin), Source (project.properties / manifest global / manifest persona).
  2. Fill in rows for at least 15 properties across these categories:
    • Logging: logger levels (DEBUG in dev, INFO in staging, WARN in production).
    • CronJobs: loadonstartup (false on api aspect, true on backgroundProcessing). Specific CronJob schedules (more frequent in dev for testing, standard in production).
    • Solr: Solr endpoint URL (different per environment).
    • Cache: cache sizes (small in dev, production-scale in staging/production).
    • Feature flags: a custom feature flag enabled in dev/staging, disabled in production until go-live.
    • Security: CORS allowed origins (localhost in dev, staging domain in staging, production domain in production).
  3. For each property, indicate where it should be defined: in an extension's project.properties (for defaults), in the manifest global section (for shared values), or in the manifest with a persona (for environment/aspect-specific values).
  4. Identify any properties that should NOT go in the manifest (e.g. database credentials managed by SAP in CCV2).

Bonus Challenge

Identify 3 properties that could cause production incidents if set incorrectly (e.g. DEBUG logging in production, CronJobs disabled on background nodes, wrong CORS origin blocking the storefront). For each, write a one-sentence explanation of the impact and how to verify the value is correct post-deployment.

Validation

Your property matrix should cover all critical configuration areas with no conflicts. Verify: no property is set at the wrong precedence level, environment-specific values use the persona field correctly, and sensitive values are not in source control.

sections.title: Exercise 4 — Release Readiness Checklist
sections.content:
Objective

Create a release readiness checklist that you would follow when SAP deploys a new quarterly CCV2 release to your non-productive environments.

Scenario

SAP has just deployed the new quarterly release to your dev and staging tenants. Production will receive it in 2 weeks. You need to verify compatibility and prepare.

Steps

  1. Read the release notes. Find the release notes for the latest Commerce Cloud version. For each section, categorise changes as: new features (evaluate), breaking changes (act), deprecated features (plan), and API changes (test).
  2. Build verification: Create a checklist of build-time checks:
    • Does the project compile against the new platform version?
    • Are any deprecated APIs used in your custom code?
    • Do all extensions load successfully (check HAC → Extensions)?
  3. Functional regression: Create a test matrix covering critical user journeys:
    • Product search and browse (Solr + catalog).
    • Add to cart and cart calculation (pricing + promotions).
    • Full checkout flow (address, delivery, payment, order placement).
    • Order management (order status, cancellation, return).
    • Backoffice product editing and CMS management.
    • Custom functionality (your LoyaltyCard features, custom endpoints).
  4. Integration verification: Check all integration points:
    • OCC API responses — compare a sample of responses before and after the upgrade for structural changes.
    • Hot Folder imports — verify files are still processed correctly.
    • Outbound integrations — verify order export, event publishing still work.
  5. Performance baseline: Run key performance tests (product search response time, cart calculation time, checkout duration) before and after to detect regressions.
  6. Go/No-Go decision: Based on your findings, create a one-page summary recommending whether to allow the production upgrade to proceed, defer, or proceed with documented workarounds.

Validation

Your checklist should be reusable for every quarterly release. It covers build, functional, integration, and performance dimensions. The go/no-go summary template can be presented to a technical lead or client stakeholder.
